sentences.text: So what happened-- I am still, as I said, I was teaching English.
And I was delighted to work in the hotel, except there was one disturbing thing in there.
The one who ran the office of the hotel was an enormous Jew-hater and Nazi.
But his girlfriend-- who was a Christian, and who was an old friend of mine-- she actually brought me to his hotel.
And he really had the feeling that he should kill me.
And only thanks to his girlfriend, who talked her into it to leave me alone.
Because I don't hurt anybody.
And so it was a very bad feeling.
But I could work without him hurting me.
And so March was coming up.
No, we still had-- so actually, life didn't change.
There was plenty of food.
I had plenty of friends.
I had some Italian friends who was a band director there.
And he had children.
And we constantly went out-- gypsy music and dinners.
And we had a good life.
That's all I can say.
There was plenty food, plenty everything until that Sunday when a very excited neighbor came in to us.
And he says to my father, Arnol, the BBC.
Put on the BBC.
Horthy is coming on the BBC.
And he will tell the world that he is now going with the Allies.
So my father ran, brought the champagne out and the glasses.
And I turned to him.
And I says, Papa, don't believe everything they say.
He says, oh, you're too young.
You don't believe in things.
So Papi pouring the liquor-- and the BBC comes.
And our admiral comes.
And he went so far as to say, proudly, I am saying it today that we are now going with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Allied Forces.
And in that moment, they cut him off.
The German horse whistle came on.
I dropped the glass.
And I ran out of the house.
And as I came out-- it was a gorgeous day.
I remember that.
It was sunny.
And the whole sky was full of black airplanes.
And I said to myself, the Americans and the British are here.
They're here!
And I ran all the way to hotel.
And when I got in, the porter said, please go up to Mr.
Merenchi ?]'s.
That's-- that's the manager.
And when I came up, he took me in his arm.
And he says, Aggie-- in English-- these are not the British.
These are the Germans.
And Horthy is already arrested.
And I want you to go home.
I don't want you to be taken from here.
So I ran all the way home.
This was sort of a shock.
Now what's going to happen?
And my father looked at me.
He said, sorry, child.
You were right.
And then we were sitting on the BBC.
But here was the Germans.
Now, funnily enough, they didn't come to pick us up.
Papi was taken once.
But as I told you, a Christian brought him home.
And right away, it was told that we have to put on the stars.
On the radio.
The radio constantly told us what has to be.
And the man has to go to labor camps.
And it was horrifying.
My hotel business--
He was in a labor camp, but only outside Budapest, not very far.
And I called up one of our Christian aristocrat friends.
He said, I'll bring papa home.
And he did immediately.
I don't know how he did it.
But he came, and he came home.
So now, of course, our life was turned around.
Because up till now, I could go to my friends who were in-- every Friday night I went to put on the lights because she couldn't do it.
And it was wonderful.
And in the house, there was a leather shop.
And the owner fall in love with my friend's daughter, the older one.
And when they started to say that married people will not be taken and all that, then he offered my friend to marry my little friend, [?
Alice, ?] who was 16 year old.
She was besides herself because she wasn't in love with him.
But he was madly in love.
And then he wanted them to come.
He looked very Christian.
So he went with his mother and brother to a very Christian neighborhood in Budapest.
And somehow, I managed to meet him every Friday.
He brought kosher food-- I still don't know where he got it-- [LAUGHS] to take it to my friends because they only had eaten Orthodox food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But any day, we didn't know what happens.
I couldn't work in the hotel.
I couldn't really give English lessons.
I tried to go to, to Anette's place still.
And then--
Reading and writing because I was always a nut for that.
And my parents, too.
They were just home.
And we were not disturbed yet.
And maybe you read in my book how-- what a wonderful super we had.
At that time, he still behaved because, I guess, Papa still gave him something.
But one, one day, I wrote a poetry.
And I send it to an editor of our greatest-- like, the And the man who ran it was one of the most famous editors of Hungary.
And I didn't send it to him.
I sent it to someone else.
And the mail brought two letters.
And one was from him.
And I have the letter.
It's handwritten.
And he says, your poetry is wonderful.
But we cannot put it in because the Center wouldn't allow it.
It was sort of against the war.
But I'm keeping it as my great treasure.
And I screamed.
And my mother was in the bus and said, what happened?
I said, Mommy, I got the Nobel Prize-- for me, it was like a Nobel Prize that this man would write me handwritten letter.
I didn't even look at the other one.
And then, when I finally laughed-- and being very happy, then the second letter came.
And the second letter was from the Swedish Embassy.
And they asked me to come and visit them.
And I turned to my mommy, and I said, Mommy, why would the Swedish Embassy want me?
She said, well, you have so many Swedish friends, and you are corresponding with them.
I said, yeah, but what they want from me?
So I didn't know what to do.
But, as I told you, my friend took me there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I stayed there.
They kept me for three weeks.
Why did they do that?
Well, [SIGHS] I arrived there.
And it turned out that the Swedish king has proclaimed all over Sweden that anyone who has friends, family, or business friends, they should come to the foreign department and tell them.
And they will try to help them out of Hungary, or, at least, while they're there.
Any Hungarian.
And this friend of mine with whom I corresponded for 15 years-- I was a little girl when I started Girl Scouting and helping.
And I never thought to take him seriously [LAUGHS] or anything, you know, because I had so many boyfriends around me.
And he-- then he explained-- no, he told me first-- the ambassador told me what the Danish king has done for the Danish Jews, that he had even put on a yellow thing.
And he said, I don't have Jews in my country.
I only have Danes, and so forth.
And then he said, and your fiance.
I said, my what?
[LAUGHS] He said your fiance went to the foreign department.
And he told them that you already would be married, but because of the war, you couldn't.
And therefore, we have to take care of you because you're a Swedish citizen.
And I stood there, I said, huh, [LAUGHS] you know?
I was shocked.
I mean, this is out of nowhere.
And he said, yes.
That's what it is.
And we want you to stay here.
I said, yes, but I have my parents.
He said, we sent-- to your parents, we sent one of our diplomats.
And we asked to bring some clothing for you.
And eventually, we will help them, too.
But first you have to be here.
Because that was the only way they could take care of me.
And I wasn't alone.
Because I was a Swedish citizen.
They couldn't help me there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they just felt that I have to be in there with them.
There was another lady-- there was a German woman who was married to a Swede with two little boys.
There was the famous vitamin-- the Nobel Prize-winning vitamin-- Szent-Gyorgyi.
I don't know if you heard his name.
He was there with me.
But he had to be removed after, with another face and name, because the Germans knew that.
And they were after him because he was a great anti-Nazi Christian.
I was-- the danger for my parents, because I wasn't in danger anymore.
Well, my parents were there.
But then my parents were so happy that I lived and that they will take care.
And, of course, every day, the diplomat went down to my parents to see if they're all right.
And they were still in the Christian house then.
But then the bombing started.
And that was the day one American was shot down and fell into the Danube.
And we saw it from where we were.
Because we were on the Buda side and the German headquarters were behind us, shooting.
And that poor guy fell into the water with a parachute.
And they took him out and lynched him.
And that did it.
Then the Jews have to be dying and the Arrow Cross said that every second house they're going to make a Jewish house.
So in Budapest, every second house had a big Magen David.
Because, they said, now the Allied forces won't bomb Budapest because they think they will kill the Jews.
How stupid can they be?
From up there, they can't see which one was a Jewish house and which one is a Christian house.
And the, the Jewish people have to get out of the Christian house.
So my parents had to move.
So my friend who took me up there came up.
And he said, she has to come home.
And the ambassador said, we are responsible for her.
We can't let her go.
And I turned to him.
I said, please, sir, if my parents are in danger, I better go.
And my friend said, the, the, the Hungarian Nazi are coming in with a list every day reading the names.
And my name is still on it.
And if they don't find--
That I have to be there.
But if they--
If I--
To be sure that all the Jews are together.
But if they wouldn't have found my name, they would have killed my parents.
Because-- where is she?
No.
I found out from our friend.
So the only thing the ambassador asked-- he said, do me a favor.
You go home by yourself.
And I send her down with a diplomat.
And we went down in the streetcar.
One side he was sitting, the other one I was sitting, and-- with a star.
I had to put a star dress back on.
And I-- of course, I wasn't out on the street for the whole three weeks.
I was just at the embassy compound.
And when we got to my house, he wrote up the address.
And he did like this.
And the only promise I had to give, whenever they allow us to go out-- because we were allowed out one hour a day to shop for milk or bread, one hour a day.
The Jews.
The afternoon between 2:00 and 3:00.
I can't remember the hour, but--
--that was not--
Frightened.
Because you didn't know who's going to shoot you in a minute.
And yet--
Yes.
And pick them up and take them.
Not yet, not while I still had the star.
When I was lucky enough to have the star removed, then I saw horrifying things.
But this comes a little bit later.
So right-- I had to promise that when I am allowed to go out, I call.
So I was down with my parents, who, thanks God, moved into my grandmother's house-- building.
Granny wasn't alive anymore.
But her daughter, the daughter's daughter-- my cousin who is now living in Canada-- and a friend of theirs with a Christian friend-- and this guy also-- not moved in, but he was constantly there.
And one day when I called up, they said, please come up.
There is something new happened.
And he took me up there again with the promise I won't stay.
And I didn't.
[LAUGHS] There was Raoul Wallenberg.
Now, I was his first helper because I told him that I was already three weeks on the day-- wonderful wings.
And I can help him.
So Per Anger and Raoul, the next morning, said to me, now you go to the police department.
And they will take off your star.
And I looked at him, and I said, alone?
He said, we'll be out in the car if something happens.
But we want you to go.
You have to.
Evidently, they must have paid big money to the police chief.
Because he was famous of killing people.
And I had this piece of paper.
And I walked in there.
Of course, my stomach went like that.
[LAUGHS] I was scared stiff.
But I went with my head up and walked in to the police chief.
And he took me in his arm.
And he cried, how proud I am that I am the one who cutting off your star.
And good luck, my child.
And will you help me when they're occupying?
And I did like we do here-- sure, I will help you.
And as I walk out, I hear somebody saying, there goes another bloody Jew.
Because they saw me coming in with a star.
But I made it.
So now I come back.
And again, they're laughing at me.
He says, so now you have another job to do.
I says, what now?
He says, the housing department.
Now, we never had a housing department.
But what happened when the Jews went out, their former maids took over their apartment and their fur coats.
And whatever there was, everybody else took it.
So I didn't even know that that-- the apartment was actually in my father's name.
But Raoul said, now, you go to the housing department.
And you get your apartment back, please.
I said, yes, sir.
What else?
I dressed up in my best suit, like, you know, my nose up.
And here, I had the paper.
And he also must have paid them because I don't think without bribe he could do that to the Hungarians.
I don't know how he did it.
I never found out.
And I walked in.
And it was full of people there.
And I was really nasty.
I just threw that paper to him.
And I figured if he kills-- if they kill me anyhow, so-- doesn't matter where it happens.
He gave me a very dirty look.
But he signed and gave it back to me.
And I was shocked.
And I walked out on the street.
And I said why am I so lucky?
Why?
Why am I getting this all?
So I walked to my old home.
And our super's wife cried, you're alive!
Darling, you're alive!
I said, yes, of course.
And now they want me to have our apartment back.
Whereby the super says, our daughter has the apartment already, OK?
So I don't know what he did to his wife because her answer was, but she has another apartment, [LAUGHS] too.
So the super says, well, anyhow, I, I have no authority.
You have to go to our lawyer.
I said, sure.
I'm going.
So again, I walked all the way to the lawyer.
And the lawyer said, well, I give you the apartment if you promise me that you will see all the good things-- what a good man I was.
And again, I said, yes.
Yes, of course, I will help you.
And I walked home.
And I gave it to the super.
So we walk up together to our apartment.
There were already a few very strange faces that we never saw in the house.
So some people got, you know, false papers.
And they moved, and so forth.
So I just found out then.
But anyhow, we walked into my apartment.
And the whole apartment was empty except the maid's room.
That was locked.
And he came.
He said, I open it for you.
And he opens it and everything was in there-- all our paintings, the books, everything.
So first I thought to myself, gee, he isn't so bad as I thought.
But then I heard him saying, he says, I'll get you yet.
OK.
So then I moved into the apartment.
Now I wanted my parents to come home at once.
And Raoul gave us-- I don't know if you saw the papers-- the Schutzpass.
Will you describe the Schutzpass, what that is?
The Schutzpass was a piece of paper.
After Raoul got 350 people helping, Christians and Jews, because, you know, Budapest is a very small city.
And it went like a wildfire that there is an angel who is helping.
And he came with a list of people, the Jewish organizations in Budapest whom he contacted.
And so they worked together.
And first, his idea was that he can take the Jews out of Budapest.
So he went to the top Hungarian Arrow Cross man.
And he offered them to take the Jews off their hand.
And he said--
This was-- well, he came in, in July.
And this was about the end of August.
Yes.
And it's about the end of--
--August--
So--
Save Hungarians.
But in the meantime--
--these 350 people also offered their apartments and their villas on the two side of the Danube to the Swedish Embassy.
And you will hear that all the time-- they're lying about it.
They said that he bought all those houses.
And he paid all the helpers.
He never bought any house because he thought he helps the Jews to keep those houses.
So they only made kiddings that the Swedish Embassy was the greatest landowners of Budapest.
Because the Swedish flag went out of the houses.
And we had a Christian or a Jewish boy who looked very Christian in Nazi uniform-- because many Nazis died already-- and taking care of our Swedish houses.
But it was still empty.
Not yet, no.
That's what he asked them, that I take them out.
I take them with a train through Germany into Sweden.
And they said, fine, you can take 5,000 people.
Now, you probably always hear the people saying this is a passport.
This is-- it is not.

Schutzpass means exactly 'protective pass.' And on the protective pass, in two languages-- in German and in Hungarian-- it said that the 'above holder of this paper and all his belongings are protected by the Swedish government.
And when and if we can collectively leave for Sweden, then in Sweden, this paper will be invalid in 14 days.' That's what it says.
The signature on it is our ambassador.
At that time, they called him Minister Danielsson, not Raoul Wallenberg.
And then, first of all, he gave it to all his helpers' parents.
So, like, my parents got it.
And then I went back to my parents.
And I said, out!
And Papi said, but, but we have that.
I said, just come home.
Let's, let's see what's happened.
I don't want you in this Jewish house.
Here, you have the paper of Raoul's.
Let's go.
They came home.
Thanks God, nothing happened then.
But then my cousin came, too.
The mother didn't want her to leave the house yet.
But my cousin, she also came home.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Raoul went to the Germans.
So I don't know how much he paid them or what.
But he was-- he spoke perfect German.
And the Germans didn't have an embassy anymore.
So the foreign-- the neutral countries took care of their stuff.
And when Raoul wanted something-- he was 7 foot tall.
And he said in German to them, if you don't do what I'm telling you, then we will not take care of your stuff.
Well, they get mail, the Germans.
And we had to take care of it.
He wouldn't give it to them.
You don't do what I want, you don't get anything.
Actually, he--
--was the sixth-- he, [LAUGHS] he was the sixth secretary.
And when he first went to speak to these, then the ambassador said, Raoul, I think I should go and speak. because I am a diplomat.
And our Raoul laughed.
He said, sir, you will be a dead talk as a diplomat.
And I will not.
And so he took over, really, the whole embassy.
At first, they didn't like it.
But then our ambassador helped us a lot with that.
Guts.
And then he picked up the people because here, we had the houses.
And he put them in these safe houses.
And they're supposed to be safe in there.
About--
--by, by this time, let's say, by September, we already had about 20,000 people.
But then we had another 10,000.
But the problem was that none of us, and not him especially-- sleeping two, three hours a night because he had to go constantly now.
Eichmann decided that-- you see, before Raoul arrived, Eichmann was there three months before him.
And he killed 475,000 people in the country-- priests, nuns, Jews, people in the wheelchair, mentally retarded people, [NON-ENGLISH], and took them.
So those who were in the country, those, those Jews were-- You just interviewed a Hungarian lady.
I don't know who did.
She just told me.
She was in the country, and she was taken to Auschwitz.
And she survived.
And the film crew was just out doing it.
But she-- so Raoul had really no choice because then the bombing started, and going away from Hungary was impossible.
And then there was a Polish gentleman who came in.
And he started to imitate these same things.
He had a little printing machine.
And Raoul found out.
And he was very, very angry.
Because by that time, the Germans allowed to make a huge, big paper out of this and had it on the walls of Budapest like the king did in Sweden, so everybody could see.
And sure enough, the Germans let people go with this paper, but not the Hungarians.
They killed people.
I was witnessing one day.
And that was more horror for me than anything.
Because a child, a little child with a star, maybe two and a half-year-old-- and evidently was lost, no parents.
And there was a church.
And out came a Catholic priest, wearing inside his white-- the Hungarian Nazi collar.
And he had a revolver.
And he saw that little kid and shot him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I felt-- if I just could shoot back.
If I can just shoot him.
But I couldn't do a thing, just picked up the child--
And he was dead.
I took it to Raoul because by that time, Raoul had an orphanage.
And I took it there.
But the child was dead.
And we couldn't find no identification.
We had no idea who it is.
We had to bury it somewhere.
And there was-- constantly, you see that all the way.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Raoul went-- you see, Eichmann decided to walk the people from Budapest all the way to the Austrian border, what even today in a car is four-and-a-half hours.
It was snowing heavily already the end of September.
But that's what he wanted.
He made trenches, so people died in the trenches.
And he didn't take us girls ever.
He took diplomats and the Red Cross.
But by that time, he went to the Swiss Red Cross, too, and asked them to help.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the Swiss started to help.
So the Swiss and the Swedish Red Cross came.
And the people died on the road.
And Raoul screamed.
He says, anybody with my paper, turn around!
And some people had his paper.
Some people didn't.
But they turned around anyhow.
And he says, go!
Go!
Get into the trucks!
And he brought a thousand people back to our houses.
And by that time, some of the young people learned from him.
And they did the tricks, just like, if Raoul can do it, we can do it.
